q0100 · root-cause-synthesis · A:correct B:correct · What are the common root causes behind API-related bugs in the BUGS project?
Expected themes: inconsistent timezone handling, datetime format inconsistency, rate limiter miscounting requests, REST API design inconsistencies

Gemini 3 + Custom MCP · correct · 14.0s · 0 tool calls

Based on an analysis of the API-related issues in the **BUGS** project, the common root causes and recurring themes are as follows:

### 1. Inconsistent Timezone and Datetime Handling
The most prevalent issue involves how the API processes and returns date/time information.
*   **Root Cause**: Lack of a unified serialization standard. Some endpoints return UTC with the 'Z' suffix, while others return server local time without any timezone indicator.
*   **Impact**: This causes ambiguity and bugs for integrations operating in different timezones.
*   **Related Issues**: 
    *   **[BUGS-97](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-97)**: Fix API returning inconsistent timezone handling.
    *   **[BUGS-98](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-98)**: Audit for datetime field consistency.
    *   **[BUGS-99](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-99)** & **[BUGS-100](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-100)**: Efforts to implement ISO 8601 UTC normalization and input parsing.

### 2. Rate Limiting Logic Errors (CORS/Preflight)
There is a specific logic flaw in how the API manages request quotas for web-based clients.
*   **Root Cause**: The rate limiter incorrectly counts **CORS OPTIONS preflight requests** toward the user's quota.
*   **Impact**: Single-page applications (SPAs) consume their rate limit twice as fast as expected (one for the preflight, one for the actual request), leading to premature 429 errors.
*   **Related Issue**: 
    *   **[BUGS-85](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-85)**: Fix rate limiter incorrectly counting OPTIONS preflight requests.

### 3. Lack of Standardization and Documentation
Several issues point to a broader "Developer Experience" (DX) gap where the API lacks consistent resource design and accessible documentation.
*   **Root Cause**: Fragmented API growth without a centralized design standard or interactive documentation portal.
*   **Related Issues**:
    *   **[BUGS-77](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-77)**: Design and implement consistent REST API v2 resource naming.
    *   **[BUGS-93](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-93)** & **[BUGS-94](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-94)**: Initiatives to set up OpenAPI (Swagger) documentation and a developer portal.

### 4. Cross-Tab Coordination Failures
In browser-based environments, the API interaction layer lacks synchronization between multiple open tabs.
*   **Root Cause**: Absence of a shared communication layer for token re

Cited: BUGS-100, BUGS-3, BUGS-77, BUGS-85, BUGS-93, BUGS-94, BUGS-97, BUGS-98

Judge: The answer directly addresses all four expected themes with specific issue citations: inconsistent timezone handling (BUGS-97-100), datetime format inconsistency (covered under timezone section), rate limiter miscounting requests (BUGS-85 specifically mentioning CORS preflight counting), and REST API design inconsistencies (BUGS-77, BUGS-93-94), plus adds additional relevant findings about cross-tab coordination.

Claude Code + Rovo MCP · correct · 6.2s · 2 tool calls

API-related bugs in BUGS share four recurring root causes: (1) inconsistent timezone/datetime handling across endpoints (BUGS-97, BUGS-98, BUGS-99, BUGS-100) with some fields in UTC, others in server-local time, others without TZ indicator; (2) rate limiter design flaws that count CORS OPTIONS preflight requests against quota, causing users to hit limits at half their actual request volume (BUGS-85, BUGS-86, BUGS-87); (3) inconsistent REST API design in v1 (mixed singular/plural, query vs path params, non-uniform error envelopes, mixed camel/snake_case) requiring a v2 redesign (BUGS-77, BUGS-76); (4) documentation drift / lack of OpenAPI source-of-truth and missing code examples / interactive console (BUGS-93, BUGS-94, BUGS-78). The unifying theme is accumulated v1 technical debt and missing standardization layers (datetime serializer, rate-limit middleware, OpenAPI spec).

Cited: BUGS-97, BUGS-98, BUGS-99, BUGS-100, BUGS-85, BUGS-86, BUGS-87, BUGS-77

Judge: The answer comprehensively addresses all four expected themes (timezone handling, datetime inconsistency, rate limiter miscounting, REST API design issues) with specific issue citations and provides additional relevant insights about documentation drift and technical debt.

q0101 · root-cause-synthesis · A:correct B:correct · Analyze the mobile app issues in BUGS - what underlying technical problems are causing user-facing symptoms?
Expected themes: scroll performance jank / frame drops, excessive battery drain from background sync, network requests failing silently, rich text rendering on main thread

Gemini 3 + Custom MCP · correct · 15.7s · 0 tool calls

Based on an analysis of the mobile-related issues in the **BUGS** project, the underlying technical problems causing user-facing symptoms can be categorized into four main areas:

### 1. Resource Management & Performance
Several issues stem from inefficient handling of device resources (CPU, Memory, Battery), leading to crashes and "jank."
*   **Memory Exhaustion (iOS):** **[BUGS-52](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-52)** causes crashes when viewing large attachments (>15MB) because the app attempts to load the entire file into memory rather than streaming or chunking it.
*   **Main Thread Blocking:** **[BUGS-72](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-72)** identifies that rich text rendering and synchronous image loading are happening on the main thread, causing frame rates to drop to 15-20fps during scrolling.
*   **Inefficient Background Processes:** **[BUGS-60](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-60)** reveals excessive battery drain (15-20% daily) due to background syncs running every 5 minutes, unnecessary location service requests, and unclosed WebSocket connections.

### 2. Network Resilience & Connectivity
The app lacks robust handling for real-world network conditions (3G, spotty WiFi, or offline environments).
*   **Silent Failures:** **[BUGS-56](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-56)** shows that the Android app "hangs" on poor connections because network timeouts (OkHttp) are not handled at the UI layer, leaving users with an infinite loading spinner.
*   **Lack of Offline Support:** **[BUGS-68](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-68)** highlights that users cannot create issues without an active connection, a critical gap for field engineers in low-connectivity areas.

### 3. Navigation & State Synchronization
Problems with how the app handles external triggers (notifications) and session state.
*   **Deep Link Race Conditions:** **[BUGS-64](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-64)** causes push notifications to open the wrong issue on Android. This is a technical timing issue where the deep link handler executes before the navigation stack is fully initialized.
*   **Token Refresh Race Conditions:** **[BUGS-2](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-2)** leads to intermittent logouts when multiple concurrent requests trigger a token refresh, invalidating the refresh token for subsequent requests.

### 4. OS-Specific Integration Failures
Issues arising from updates to native libraries or OS security requi

Cited: BUGS-18, BUGS-2, BUGS-52, BUGS-56, BUGS-60, BUGS-64, BUGS-68, BUGS-72

Judge: The answer provides an excellent, well-structured analysis of mobile app issues with specific technical root causes, directly addressing all expected themes (scroll jank/frame drops via BUGS-72, battery drain from background sync via BUGS-60, silent network failures via BUGS-56, and rich text rendering on main thread via BUGS-72), plus additional relevant technical problems with proper issue citations.

Claude Code + Rovo MCP · correct · 5.4s · 1 tool calls

Mobile user-facing symptoms in BUGS map to specific underlying technical problems: (1) scroll jank and 15-20fps drops (BUGS-72) caused by markdown/rich-text rendering on the main thread, synchronous avatar image loads, uncached cell-height calculation, and 50+ live cells held in memory; (2) excessive battery drain (BUGS-60) caused by background sync running every 5 min regardless of settings, radio waking with no new data, an unused legacy location permission, and a WebSocket kept alive while backgrounded; (3) silent network failures on poor connections (BUGS-56) where OkHttp's 60s timeout is not surfaced to the UI, no retry/backoff, no offline detection; (4) iOS attachment crashes (BUGS-52) from loading entire 15MB+ files into memory instead of using QuickLook/streaming; (5) MFA crashes on Android 12+ (BUGS-18) due to biometric API version handling. Root themes: main-thread heavy rendering, no battery-aware scheduling, missing UX for transient/timeout failures, and unbounded memory loads.

Cited: BUGS-72, BUGS-60, BUGS-56, BUGS-52, BUGS-18, BUGS-51, BUGS-61, BUGS-63

Judge: The answer provides excellent grounded analysis mapping user-facing symptoms to underlying technical causes, directly addressing all expected themes (scroll jank from main-thread rendering, battery drain from background sync, silent network failures) plus additional valid issues like memory management and API compatibility, with specific issue citations and root cause identification.

q0102 · root-cause-synthesis · A:correct B:correct · Looking at CRM issues, what patterns indicate systemic gaps in the customer support workflow?
Expected themes: lack of refund status visibility, missing engineering escalation path, account lockout self-service gaps, incident-to-ticket linking missing

Gemini 3 + Custom MCP · correct · 10.9s · 0 tool calls

Based on an analysis of the 100 issues in the **CRM** project, several systemic gaps in the customer support workflow are evident. The issues are largely focused on building infrastructure to solve these recurring manual bottlenecks:

### 1. High Support Volume from Account Recovery
A significant portion of the workflow is currently burdened by manual account recovery and identity verification.
*   **The Gap**: Lack of self-service options for locked accounts or lost credentials.
*   **Evidence**: Issues like **[CRM-26](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-26)** (Modernize Account Recovery Flow to Reduce Support Volume) and **[CRM-35](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-35)** (Automated Account Lockout Detection) indicate that agents are currently handling these high-frequency, low-complexity tasks manually.

### 2. Manual Billing & Refund Processing
The workflow appears to suffer from a lack of automation in financial disputes, leading to slow resolution times.
*   **The Gap**: Agents are manually retrieving transaction evidence and processing refunds across different payment gateways.
*   **Evidence**: **[CRM-2](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-2)** (Automated Transaction Evidence Retrieval) and **[CRM-51](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-51)** (Improve Refund Processing Efficiency) suggest that the current process is fragmented and requires manual data entry across Stripe, PayPal, and Braintree.

### 3. Technical Context "Blindness"
Support agents likely struggle to diagnose technical issues because they lack immediate access to the customer's environment or crash data.
*   **The Gap**: A "silo" between support tickets and engineering data (crashes/logs).
*   **Evidence**: **[CRM-76](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-76)** (Enhance App Crash and Technical Issue Reporting) and **[CRM-89](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-89)** (Customer Technical Environment Collection) show a push to automate the gathering of technical context that is currently missing from initial reports.

### 4. Reactive vs. Proactive Communication
The current workflow is reactive, meaning customers only get updates when they ask or when a human intervenes.
*   **The Gap**: No automated status tracking or proactive notification for known shipping/billing delays.
*   **Evidence**: **[CRM-56](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-56)** (Customer-Facing Refund Status Tracking Portal) and **[CRM-64](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-64)**

Cited: CRM-18, CRM-2, CRM-26, CRM-35, CRM-51, CRM-56, CRM-64, CRM-76

Judge: The answer provides a thorough, well-structured analysis of systemic gaps in the CRM customer support workflow, directly addressing expected themes including refund status visibility (CRM-56), account lockout self-service gaps (CRM-26, CRM-35), and incident-to-ticket linking (CRM-76, CRM-89), with strong evidence from specific issues and actionable summary recommendations.

Claude Code + Rovo MCP · correct · 6.1s · 2 tool calls

CRM patterns reveal four systemic support-workflow gaps: (1) no customer-facing visibility into refund status, generating 15% of agent volume in 'where is my refund' follow-ups (CRM-56, CRM-57, CRM-58, CRM-59); (2) no formal engineering escalation path - reproducible bugs are tracked in spreadsheets, ad-hoc emails, and Slack mentions, averaging 2 weeks to acknowledgment (CRM-81, CRM-82, CRM-83, CRM-84); (3) account lockout self-service gaps - ~2,000 lockouts/day with no self-unlock, forcing email-to-support (CRM-35, CRM-37, CRM-38); (4) no incident-to-ticket linking, causing duplicate triage during outages (CRM-93, CRM-95, CRM-96). Underlying systemic issue: support, engineering, and incident-management tools are siloed; customer-facing transparency is missing.

Cited: CRM-56, CRM-81, CRM-35, CRM-93, CRM-95, CRM-96, CRM-82, CRM-83

Judge: The answer comprehensively addresses all four expected themes with specific issue citations and quantified data: refund status visibility (15% agent volume), missing engineering escalation (2-week acknowledgment), account lockout self-service gaps (~2,000/day), and incident-to-ticket linking issues, plus identifies the underlying systemic cause of tool silos.

q0103 · root-cause-synthesis · A:correct B:correct · What datetime and timezone issues exist across the BUGS project and what's the common technical root cause?
Expected keys: BUGS-100, BUGS-99, BUGS-98, BUGS-97
Expected count: 4
Oracle JQL: project = BUGS AND (summary ~ datetime OR summary ~ timezone OR description ~ "UTC")
Expected themes: inconsistent timezone handling, server timezone vs UTC, missing timezone indicator, ISO 8601 standardization needed

Gemini 3 + Custom MCP · correct · 8.9s · 0 tool calls

There are currently 4 active issues in the **BUGS** project related to datetime and timezone handling.

### **Identified Issues**
1.  **[BUGS-97](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-97): Fix API returning inconsistent timezone handling**
    *   The API currently returns a mix of UTC with the 'Z' suffix, server local time without a timezone indicator, and ambiguous "date-only" fields. This causes integration failures for clients in different timezones.
2.  **[BUGS-98](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-98): Audit all API responses for datetime field consistency**
    *   A systematic review is needed to identify database columns storing local time instead of UTC and to categorize the risk level of existing inconsistencies.
3.  **[BUGS-99](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-99): Implement datetime serializer with ISO 8601 UTC output**
    *   The project needs a standardized JSON serializer to ensure all outgoing API datetimes use the ISO 8601 format with a `Z` suffix.
4.  **[BUGS-100](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-100): Add input datetime normalization**
    *   Incoming data is not being normalized correctly. The system needs to accept multiple formats (ISO 8601, Unix, etc.) but convert everything to UTC internally while logging warnings for non-UTC inputs.

---

### **Common Technical Root Cause**
The common root cause is a **lack of a unified datetime standard across the application layers**. Specifically:

*   **Inconsistent Serialization**: The application lacks a global serializer, leading to different endpoints formatting dates based on local server settings or default library behaviors.
*   **Ambiguous Storage/Retrieval**: Some database columns are likely storing "wall-clock time" (local time) without timezone offsets, making it impossible to determine the exact moment in time once the data is retrieved by a client in a different region.
*   **Missing Normalization on Ingress**: The system is "trusting" the input format of incoming API requests rather than forcing a conversion to a single internal standard (UTC) at the boundary.

Cited: BUGS-100, BUGS-97, BUGS-98, BUGS-99

Judge: asked for clarification

Claude Code + Rovo MCP · correct · 4.8s · 1 tool calls

Datetime/timezone issues in BUGS span four issues forming a single epic-style cluster: BUGS-97 (Story, High) 'Fix API returning inconsistent timezone handling for date fields' is the parent problem - some endpoints return UTC with Z suffix, others return server-local time without indicator, date-only fields are TZ-ambiguous. Subtasks: BUGS-98 'Audit all API responses for datetime field consistency' (script all endpoints, identify columns storing local time needing migration), BUGS-99 'Implement datetime serializer with ISO 8601 UTC output', BUGS-100 'Add input datetime normalization with deprecation warnings'. Common technical root cause: absence of a centralized serializer/normalizer; datetimes are formatted ad-hoc in handlers and some database columns store server-local time, so UTC-vs-local divergence accumulated over time. Fix path is ISO 8601 standardization, X-Request-Timezone header for client rendering, and DB audit/migration of any local-time columns.

Cited: BUGS-97, BUGS-98, BUGS-99, BUGS-100

Judge: The answer comprehensively addresses datetime/timezone issues with specific issue citations, identifies the exact technical root cause (absence of centralized serializer, ad-hoc formatting, local-time columns), and covers all expected themes including inconsistent timezone handling, UTC vs server timezone, missing timezone indicator, and ISO 8601 standardization as the solution path.

q0104 · root-cause-synthesis · A:correct B:correct · Based on high priority issues in CRM, what are the main security and fraud-related concerns that need addressing?
Expected keys: CRM-85, CRM-81, CRM-60, CRM-56, CRM-35, CRM-31 …(+2)
Expected count: 8
Oracle JQL: project = CRM AND priority = High
Expected themes: refund fraud detection, account takeover prevention, identity verification gaps, login lockout handling

Gemini 3 + Custom MCP · correct · 6.4s · 0 tool calls

Based on the high-priority issues in the **CRM** project, the main security and fraud-related concerns center around account protection, identity verification, and refund fraud.

### **1. Fraud Detection & Prevention**
There is a significant focus on automating the detection of fraudulent activities, particularly regarding refunds:
*   **[CRM-60](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-60)**: Development of a **Refund Fraud Detection Model** using machine learning to identify suspicious patterns.
*   **[CRM-52](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-52)**: Implementation of an **Instant Refund Eligibility Engine**, likely designed to balance customer experience with risk controls for low-risk users.

### **2. Account Security & Access Control**
Protecting user accounts from unauthorized access and streamlining recovery are top priorities:
*   **[CRM-35](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-35)**: Development of **Automated Account Lockout Detection**, which is critical for mitigating brute-force or credential-stuffing attacks.
*   **[CRM-27](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-27)**: Implementation of **SMS-Based Account Recovery** to provide a secure alternative for users to regain access to their accounts.

### **3. Identity Verification**
To mitigate risks associated with high-value or suspicious transactions, the team is enhancing verification protocols:
*   **[CRM-31](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-31)**: Creation of an **Identity Verification Workflow** specifically for high-risk actions, ensuring an extra layer of security when it is most needed.

**Summary of Focus:**
The CRM project is currently prioritizing **proactive detection** (ML models and lockout triggers) and **strengthened authentication** (ID verification and secure recovery) to combat evolving fraud and security threats.

Cited: CRM-27, CRM-31, CRM-35, CRM-52, CRM-60

Judge: The answer comprehensively addresses all expected themes - refund fraud detection (CRM-60, CRM-52), account takeover prevention via lockout detection (CRM-35), identity verification gaps (CRM-31), and login lockout handling (CRM-35, CRM-27) - with specific issue citations and well-organized analysis.

Claude Code + Rovo MCP · correct · 4.5s · 1 tool calls

High-priority CRM issues cluster around four security/fraud concerns: (1) Refund fraud - estimated 4% rate, catching <50%; CRM-60 builds an ML model with 0-100 risk scoring, fraud-specialist queue for >75 scores, and feature inputs covering customer history, claim type, and device fingerprint; CRM-6 builds the self-service dispute portal with rate-limit (max 3/24h) abuse controls. (2) Account takeover prevention - 12 ATO incidents last quarter from phished passwords + email changes; CRM-31 mandates re-auth + dual confirmation for email changes, government-ID fallback via Jumio, and audit trail visible to support. (3) Account lockout / credential stuffing handling - CRM-35 keeps 30-min lockout for suspicious patterns (multi-IP, VPN/TOR ranges) while adding self-unlock for legitimate users. (4) Multi-transaction billing dispute escalation - CRM-10 detects related subscription charges, requires supervisor approval >$200, and adds compliance audit log. Supporting: CRM-56 (refund tracking), CRM-81 (engineering escalation), CRM-85 (automated troubleshooting). Core theme: balancing friction reduction for legitimate users with stronger verification at high-risk moments.

Cited: CRM-60, CRM-31, CRM-35, CRM-10, CRM-6, CRM-56, CRM-81, CRM-85

Judge: The answer comprehensively addresses all four expected security/fraud themes (refund fraud detection via CRM-60/CRM-6, account takeover prevention via CRM-31, identity verification via Jumio integration, and login lockout handling via CRM-35) with specific issue keys, quantified metrics, and detailed implementation approaches grounded in real CRM data.

q0105 · root-cause-synthesis · A:correct B:correct · What integration and alerting gaps exist in CRM that could be causing delayed response to customer issues?
Expected themes: PagerDuty integration needed, ticket volume monitoring gaps, anomaly detection missing, incident auto-creation from alerts

Gemini 3 + Custom MCP · correct · 6.7s · 0 tool calls

Based on the current open issues in the CRM project, there are several significant integration and alerting gaps that directly impact the speed of response to customer issues. These can be categorized into three main areas:

### 1. Lack of Automated Incident & Technical Alerting
Currently, the team lacks a direct link between technical failures and support visibility, leading to reactive rather than proactive support.
*   **[CRM-100](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-100)**: There is a pending need to create an alerting integration with PagerDuty for engineering.
*   **[CRM-95](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-95)**: Incident detection is not yet integrated with PagerDuty, which prevents support from knowing about outages before customers report them.
*   **[CRM-78](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-78)** & **[CRM-77](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-77)**: Sentry crash captures and in-app crash reports are not yet integrated with support tickets, meaning agents lack the technical context (logs/crashes) needed to resolve issues quickly.

### 2. Manual Data Retrieval & Siloed Information
Support agents are likely slowed down by having to manually check external systems for shipping and billing data.
*   **[CRM-82](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-82)**: There is no automatic bug ticket creation from CRM to Jira, requiring manual duplication of effort.
*   **[CRM-70](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-70)** & **[CRM-69](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-69)**: Integration with warehouse management and carrier APIs for return tracking is still in progress.
*   **[CRM-57](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-57)** & **[CRM-54](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-54)**: Refund status and payment provider APIs (Stripe/PayPal) are not fully integrated, forcing manual status checks.

### 3. Missing Proactive Customer Communication
The absence of automated status updates means customers must reach out to support for information that could be self-served.
*   **[CRM-93](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-93)**: The "Known Issues Status Page" with customer subscriptions is not yet built.
*   **[CRM-65](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-65)**: Shipping delay detection logic is not yet integrated with customer notifications.
*   **[CRM-14](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-14)**: Real-time chargeback alerts from payment providers are missing, delaying dispute resolution.

### Summary of Imp

Cited: CRM-100, CRM-14, CRM-54, CRM-57, CRM-65, CRM-69, CRM-70, CRM-77

Judge: The answer comprehensively addresses integration and alerting gaps with specific issue citations covering PagerDuty integration (CRM-100, CRM-95), incident auto-creation (CRM-82), and detection gaps (CRM-78, CRM-77), while also identifying additional relevant gaps in logistics and customer communication systems.

Claude Code + Rovo MCP · correct · 5.7s · 2 tool calls

CRM integration/alerting gaps that delay response: (1) No PagerDuty integration for support-detected emerging issues - CRM-100 plans P1 PagerDuty + P2 Slack alerts when ticket volume exceeds baseline. (2) No real-time ticket-volume monitoring or anomaly detection - CRM-98 builds a streaming pipeline with 5-min window aggregation segmented by category/app version/OS/geo; CRM-99 implements baseline-comparison anomaly detection; CRM-97 raises engineering PagerDuty when ticket volume exceeds 2x baseline (recent example: Android 14 login failures detected via tickets 4 hours before monitoring saw API errors because failures were client-side). (3) No incident auto-creation from PagerDuty alerts tagged 'customer-facing' (CRM-95), and no ticket-to-incident linking for duplicate prevention (CRM-96). (4) No public status page (CRM-93/CRM-94) so customers don't see known issues, flooding support with duplicates. Root cause: support telemetry runs in isolation from engineering/SRE alerting stack.

Cited: CRM-100, CRM-99, CRM-98, CRM-97, CRM-95, CRM-96, CRM-93

Judge: The answer comprehensively addresses all expected themes with specific issue citations: PagerDuty integration (CRM-100, CRM-97), ticket volume monitoring gaps (CRM-98), anomaly detection missing (CRM-99), and incident auto-creation from alerts (CRM-95, CRM-96), plus additional relevant gaps like status page absence and root cause analysis.

q0106 · root-cause-synthesis · A:correct B:correct · Examine API documentation issues in BUGS - what's causing developer confusion based on the issue descriptions?
Expected themes: missing code examples, tutorial guides needed, OpenAPI documentation setup, inconsistent endpoint naming

Gemini 3 + Custom MCP · correct · 9.3s · 0 tool calls

Based on the issue descriptions in the **BUGS** project, developer confusion regarding the API documentation stems from several core structural and content-related problems:

### 1. Static and Outdated Documentation
The primary source of frustration is that current documentation is maintained as a **static Markdown file** that is frequently out of sync with the actual API implementation.
*   **Impact**: Developers resort to "trial-and-error" or opening support tickets because they cannot trust the written docs (**[BUGS-93](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-93)**).
*   **Missing Features**: There is a lack of interactive "try it" functionality, multi-language code examples, and documentation for error scenarios (**[BUGS-93](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-93)**).

### 2. Inconsistent Resource Design (API v1)
The existing API (v1) suffers from inconsistent naming conventions and non-RESTful design, which makes it unpredictable for developers.
*   **Naming Conflicts**: Inconsistent pluralization (e.g., `/issue/{id}` vs. `/projects/{id}`) and mixed parameter styles (path params vs. query params) (**[BUGS-77](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-77)**).
*   **Error Handling**: Error responses vary wildly across endpoints, making it difficult for developers to implement consistent error-handling logic (**[BUGS-77](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-77)**).

### 3. Ambiguous Data Handling
Specific technical inconsistencies in how data is returned lead to integration bugs:
*   **Timezone Confusion**: Endpoints return dates in a mix of UTC, server time, or without any timezone indicator at all. This ambiguity causes significant issues for integrations operating in different timezones (**[BUGS-97](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-97)**).

### 4. Lack of Modern Developer Experience (DX)
The current API ecosystem is viewed as having "significant technical debt."
*   **Missing Capabilities**: Developers are hampered by a lack of modern features like webhooks and a proper developer portal (**[BUGS-76](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-76)**).
*   **Goal**: The team is currently planning an API v2 modernization initiative specifically to reduce API-related support tickets by 80% (**[BUGS-76](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-76)**).

### Summary of Issues
| Key | Summary | Root Cause of Confusion |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **[BUGS-97](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-97)** | Inconsistent timezone handling |

Cited: BUGS-76, BUGS-77, BUGS-93, BUGS-97

Judge: asked for clarification

Claude Code + Rovo MCP · correct · 4.3s · 1 tool calls

API documentation issues in BUGS show developer confusion stems from: (1) static Markdown docs that drift out of sync with implementation, forcing devs into trial-and-error and support tickets (BUGS-93); (2) lack of an OpenAPI source-of-truth - BUGS-78 creates the OpenAPI 3.1 spec for v2 with $ref reusable components and validation, BUGS-94 deploys Stoplight/Redocly auto-updated on spec changes; (3) missing code examples in multiple languages (Python/JS/Ruby/Go/cURL) and no interactive 'try it' console (BUGS-93); (4) inconsistent endpoint naming, error response formats, and pagination across v1 (BUGS-77) compounds doc confusion since the API itself isn't predictable. Underlying cause: docs are a manual artifact rather than generated, and the v1 API design itself lacks the consistency that makes docs scannable. v2 modernization (BUGS-76) bundles API consistency + spec-driven docs + dev portal as a unit.

Cited: BUGS-93, BUGS-94, BUGS-78, BUGS-77, BUGS-76

Judge: The answer thoroughly addresses API documentation issues causing developer confusion, covering all expected themes (missing code examples, OpenAPI setup, inconsistent endpoint naming) with specific issue citations and grounded analysis of root causes including static docs drifting out of sync and lack of spec-driven documentation.

q0107 · root-cause-synthesis · A:correct B:correct · What common technical debt patterns can be identified across high priority bugs in the BUGS project?
Expected keys: BUGS-97, BUGS-85, BUGS-77, BUGS-72, BUGS-60, BUGS-56 …(+4)
Expected count: 10
Oracle JQL: project = BUGS AND priority = High
Expected themes: inconsistent API design, rate limiter bugs, mobile performance issues, network error handling gaps

Gemini 3 + Custom MCP · correct · 23.8s · 0 tool calls

Based on an analysis of the high-priority issues and their associated initiatives in the **BUGS** project, several recurring technical debt patterns have emerged. These patterns indicate systemic issues in the codebase that are now manifesting as critical bugs or performance bottlenecks.

### 1. Concurrency and State Management Debt
A significant cluster of high-priority bugs stems from improper handling of asynchronous state and concurrent operations.
*   **Race Conditions**: **[BUGS-2](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-2)** (OAuth token refresh) and **[BUGS-3](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-3)** highlight a lack of cross-tab coordination and mutex handling in the authentication layer.
*   **Memory Leaks**: **[BUGS-6](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-6)** and **[BUGS-7](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-7)** point to "subscription debt," where auth state listeners are not properly cleaned up, leading to dashboard crashes.

### 2. Mobile Platform Fragmentation & Legacy APIs
The mobile applications (iOS/Android) are suffering from debt related to outdated handling of OS-specific behaviors.
*   **Version-Specific Crashes**: **[BUGS-18](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-18)** (Android 12+ MFA crashes) and **[BUGS-20](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-20)** show that the apps haven't been updated to handle modern biometric or permission APIs.
*   **Legacy Services**: **[BUGS-62](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-62)** explicitly mentions the need to remove "legacy location services and WebSocket backends" that are causing excessive battery drain (**[BUGS-60](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-60)**).

### 3. Data Consistency & Serialization Debt
There is a pattern of inconsistent data handling across the API and frontend, particularly regarding time and identity.
*   **Timezone Handling**: **[BUGS-97](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-97)**, **[BUGS-98](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-98)**, and **[BUGS-99](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-99)** reveal a lack of a unified datetime serialization strategy (ISO 8601), requiring a project-wide audit.
*   **Parsing Fragility**: **[BUGS-10](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-10)** (SAML parsing) and **[BUGS-43](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-43)** (Search syntax crashes) indicate that parsers were built without sufficient edge-case validation or iterative logic (**[BUGS-44](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/br

Cited: BUGS-10, BUGS-18, BUGS-2, BUGS-20, BUGS-27, BUGS-3, BUGS-31, BUGS-33

Judge: The answer provides a thorough, well-organized analysis of technical debt patterns from high-priority bugs with specific issue citations, covering mobile performance issues and network error handling gaps from the expected themes while also surfacing additional valid patterns like concurrency issues, data serialization debt, and infrastructure scalability problems.

Claude Code + Rovo MCP · correct · 4.0s · 1 tool calls

High-priority BUGS issues reveal a consistent technical-debt picture across 10 tickets: API-design debt - inconsistent v1 REST design (BUGS-77), datetime/timezone inconsistency (BUGS-97), rate limiter counting OPTIONS preflight (BUGS-85); mobile-platform debt - scroll-perf jank from main-thread rich-text rendering (BUGS-72), excessive battery drain from undisciplined background sync (BUGS-60), silent network failures on Android with no retry/timeout UX (BUGS-56), MFA crash from missing Android 12+ biometric API handling (BUGS-18); auth/session debt - session invalidation not propagating to mobile after password change (BUGS-14), memory leak in auth state subscription (BUGS-6); search-relevance debt - scoring ignores severity/recency (BUGS-35). Common pattern: missing centralized layers (serializer, rate-limit middleware, network/UX layer, push-based session sync) and accumulated platform-API drift that wasn't kept current.

Cited: BUGS-97, BUGS-85, BUGS-77, BUGS-72, BUGS-60, BUGS-56, BUGS-35, BUGS-18

Judge: The answer provides excellent grounded analysis identifying multiple technical debt patterns (API design inconsistency, mobile platform debt, auth/session debt, search relevance debt) with specific issue citations, directly matching expected themes like API design, rate limiter bugs, mobile performance, and network error handling while also surfacing additional valid patterns from the real data.

q0108 · root-cause-synthesis · A:correct B:correct · Looking at CRM subtasks, what operational inefficiencies are the team trying to solve?
Expected keys: CRM-100, CRM-99, CRM-98, CRM-96, CRM-95, CRM-94 …(+66)

Expected themes: ticket-to-incident linking, PagerDuty integration, anomaly detection baseline, status page evaluation

Gemini 3 + Custom MCP · correct · 14.2s · 0 tool calls

Based on the current CRM subtasks, the team is focused on solving several key operational inefficiencies through automation, improved visibility, and system integration.

### 1. Manual Support & Incident Overhead
The team is working to reduce the manual effort required to manage support tickets and technical incidents:
*   **Automated Incident Detection**: Implementing anomaly detection (**[CRM-99](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-99)**) and ticket volume monitoring (**[CRM-98](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-98)**) to catch issues before they escalate.
*   **Duplicate Prevention**: Building ticket-to-incident linking (**[CRM-96](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-96)**) to prevent agents from working on redundant issues.
*   **Context Gathering**: Automating the collection of technical environment data (**[CRM-91](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-91)**) and user journey capture (**[CRM-80](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-80)**) so agents don't have to ask customers for basic details.

### 2. Fragmented Refund & Return Workflows
A significant portion of the work is dedicated to streamlining financial operations:
*   **Programmatic Returns**: Integrating carrier APIs for labels (**[CRM-69](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-69)**) and warehouse management systems (**[CRM-70](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-70)**) to trigger auto-refunds upon confirmation.
*   **Proactive Resolution**: Building logic to detect shipping delays and proactively offer resolutions (**[CRM-65](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-65)**, **[CRM-66](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-66)**) before the customer contacts support.
*   **Bulk Actions**: Implementing bulk refund capabilities with supervisor overrides (**[CRM-12](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-12)**) to handle mass issues efficiently.

### 3. Security & Account Recovery Friction
The team is addressing inefficiencies in how users recover accounts, which typically generates high ticket volume:
*   **Self-Service Recovery**: Building self-service unlock flows (**[CRM-37](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-37)**) and backup code systems (**[CRM-44](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-44)**) to reduce reliance on manual verification.
*   **Automated Risk Scoring**: Implementing account risk scoring (**[CRM-40](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-40)**) to automate the approval of low-risk recovery attempts while flagging high-risk ones f

Cited: CRM-12, CRM-17, CRM-19, CRM-20, CRM-24, CRM-25, CRM-37, CRM-40

Judge: The answer provides a comprehensive, well-organized analysis of operational inefficiencies grounded in real CRM subtask data, covering expected themes like ticket-to-incident linking (CRM-96) and anomaly detection (CRM-99), while also surfacing additional valid themes like refund workflows, account recovery, and knowledge gaps with specific issue citations throughout.

Claude Code + Rovo MCP · correct · 5.3s · 1 tool calls

CRM subtasks (72 tickets) decompose larger initiatives into operational fixes targeting these inefficiencies: ticket-to-incident linking and duplicate prevention during outages (CRM-96, CRM-95, CRM-93/94 status page evaluation); PagerDuty + Slack alerting for ticket-volume anomalies (CRM-100, CRM-99 anomaly baseline, CRM-98 segmented monitoring); engineering escalation automation (CRM-82 Jira API integration, CRM-83 bidirectional sync, CRM-84 SLA dashboard, CRM-78 Sentry crash dedup); refund-flow automation (CRM-52/54/55 instant refunds + processor integration + monitoring, CRM-57/58/59 refund status API + UI + notifications, CRM-62 ML serving, CRM-63 fraud-specialist queue, CRM-67 reporting, CRM-71/72 warehouse + exception workflows); account-security operations (CRM-37 self-unlock email, CRM-38 lockout analytics, CRM-31 identity verification subtasks); knowledge automation (CRM-88 guide-suggestion UI, CRM-92 form data into ticket display). Underlying inefficiencies: manual triage during outages, ad-hoc engineering handoffs, unsearchable error knowledge, and slow refund processing.
